---
title: Background information
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    number-sections: true
authors: "Marta Aransay, Carlota de la Concepción, Marta Rodrigo, Lucía Santos"
date: 10/03/2026
---

# Background information

## Protein 1. ORAI1 protein

**PDB_entry**: [ORAI1](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/Q96D31/entry)

The ORAI1 protein, serves as the essential pore-forming subunit of the calcium release-activated calcium (CRAC) channel and is a critical target for structural bioinformatics studies due to its complex regulatory mechanism. This entry corresponds to the human version; however, there is a high degree of evolutionary conservation with numerous orthologs present in both vertebrates and invertebrates.. It is vital to consider its quaternary structure, as ORAI1 does not function as a monomer but assembles into **hexameric homomers** to form the CRAC channel or **heteropentamers with ORAI3** to form arachidonate-regulated calcium (ARC) channels. The protein’s topology consists of **four transmembrane helical segments** with both the **N-terminus and C-terminus located in the cytoplasm**, which is a key consideration for docking since these **terminal regions are the primary binding sites for regulatory proteins like STIM1 and STIM2**. 
>>its biological activity depends on the correct assembly of multiple subunits rather than on the structure of a single monomer.
The protein undergoes several **post-translational modifications (PTMs)**, such as N-glycosylation at D223, which regulates its functional activity, and oxidation at C195, which serves as a channel inactivation mechanism. Also, N-glycosylation inhibits channel activity in T cells

There are **two known isoforms** of this protein, **alpha** and **beta**; while the alpha isoform acts as a pore-forming subunit for both CRAC and ARC channels, the beta isoform is exclusively involved in forming CRAC channels.

Ligands: Calcium ion (Ca2+)

@@@ para citar esta foto: @fig-resolution-quality

!["Methods of resolution and qualityy"](../pics/MethodsResolution.png){#fig-resolution-quality}

The canonical isoform is the alpha isoform, which will be used for the structural modeling.



***From a modeling perspective, the initial N-terminal region (residues 1-51) is predicted to be disordered, potentially requiring specialized ab initio modeling or extended molecular dynamics to achieve a stable conformation.***

**CdlCR: Creo que esto no es tán importante porque no tiene que ver mucho con el modelaje, tipo si tiene que ver pero lo dejaria como indicado con bbl y ya así solo tendríamos que elegir un estado del ambiente y de ahí modelarlo como abierto o cerrado. No se que sería mejor si el estado oxidado y el reducido o tambien intentar simularlo en presencia de colesterol. Sinceramente yo no lo pondría, indicaría que la presencia de colesterol y pip2 afecta pero no modelaria. ponemos bbl de eso y ya. Me he repetido una barbaridad pero que no haria 25 modelajes en las distintas condiciones, indicaria los ligandos que afectan a la actividad.**
**MA: opino igual, nos dejamos de chorradas je**


### Activity regulation

The functional state of the channel is heavily influenced by specific chemical environments and molecular partners that should be accounted for in any simulation. The pore’s selectivity for calcium ions is governed by the highly conserved Glu-106 residue, and its activity can be modulated by the redox state of ***Cys-195, whose oxidation leads to channel inactivation***. Furthermore, ORAI1’s localization within lipid rafts implies that the surrounding membrane composition, particularly the presence of ***cholesterol and PIP2, significantly impacts its structural stability and gating***. In addition to ion permeation, modeling ORAI1 often involves its interactome, specifically the STIM1 protein which triggers channel opening through physical coupling, and ***Calmodulin***, which mediates calcium-dependent inactivation. Finally, incorporating known disease-associated variants such as the R91W loss-of-function mutation or the constitutively active G98S and V107M mutations can provide valuable insights into the channel's conformational landscape and its role in calcium-related pathologies. Both CRAC and ARC channels are ***inhibited by lanthanides including La3+ and Gd3+ ions***



Nota de modesto: Consider alternative quaternary structures in order to obtain the best holoprotein channel.

The **** protein has X ligand ...

## Protein 2

Seq2.1: 

blastp de >MBE6593352.1 100% de identidad y 100% query coverage con: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/protein/MBE6593352.1?report=genbank&log$=prottop&blast_rank=1&RID=V2GKFU6A014 Es la que más info tiene asique cogería esta, nos quitamos de justificar en la ultima porque una u otra y en la de cluster19 es una hypothetical protein multispecie. Lo que os querais complicar en buscar info y demás

--> la 2.1 no tiene estructura resuelta en AF ni en PDB (buscador avanzado por secuencia), aunque en PDB salen algunas opciones con un 50% de identidad
--> tampoco en uniprot porque si haces un blastp te hace match con: 

hypothetical protein [Oscillospiraceae bacterium]
Sequence ID: MBE6593352.1
Score	Expect	Method	Identities	Positives	Gaps
1288 bits(3334)	0.0	Compositional matrix adjust.	624/624(100%)	624/624(100%)	0/624(0%)
PERO Como pone hypothetical protein es que no tiene estructura crsitalizada

### Sequence Analysis 

Before initiating the 3D modeling protocols, a comprehensive 1D sequence analysis was performed to understand the structural context of the protein and anticipate potential modeling challenges.

Transmembrane Domains and Signal Peptides: Analysis using a topological predictor (DeepTMHMM) revealed a complete absence of Transmembrane Regions (TMRs). Instead, a well-defined N-terminal signal peptide was identified spanning residues 1 to 55. This strongly suggests that the target is not a membrane-resident protein, but rather a secreted molecule that functions in the extracellular or periplasmic space after the cleavage of its signal peptide.

Intrinsically Disordered Regions (IDRs): To assess local flexibility, the sequence was evaluated using IUPred3. The prediction highlighted two main regions of intrinsic disorder. The most prominent peak (score > 0.8) occurs between residues 60 and 85, immediately following the signal peptide cleavage site. This region likely acts as a highly flexible linker. A secondary disordered loop was predicted around residues 470-500. Identifying these IDRs is crucial for the subsequent model assessment, as these specific regions are expected to display low confidence scores (e.g., low pLDDT) in Deep Learning models, reflecting their natural structural flexibility rather than an algorithm failure.

> Functional Annotation and Evolutionary Insights
To infer the biological role of this uncharacterized protein, domain scanning was conducted using InterProScan. The analysis yielded highly informative matches:

Catalytic Core: A highly stable, structured central domain (residues 152-392) was confidently annotated as belonging to the Pectate Lyase family (Pectate_lyase_3 / Pectin lyase-like).

Evolutionary Link: Interestingly, InterProScan also reported structural domain matches corresponding to Phage tail proteins (Phage_tail_b).

> Biological Hypothesis
The integration of these findings provides a cohesive functional and evolutionary hypothesis. Pectate lyases and phage tail spike proteins frequently share a similar structural architecture, specifically the parallel beta-helix fold, which is highly efficient at binding and cleaving complex carbohydrates. Given that the source organism, Oscillospiraceae, is a known resident of the mammalian gut microbiome, we hypothesize that this target is a secreted enzyme. It is likely utilized by the bacterium to degrade complex plant polysaccharides (such as pectin from the host's diet) to obtain nutrients. The structural similarity to phage tail proteins suggests a fascinating evolutionary history, potentially involving a horizontal gene transfer event where a viral carbohydrate-degrading structural gene was co-opted by the bacterium for metabolic purposes.

#### Predicted Topology (DeepTMHMM)
Predicted Topologies
>Sequence | SP





##gff-version 3
# Sequence Length: 624
# Sequence Number of predicted TMRs: 0
Sequence signal 1 55
Sequence outside 56 624

## Protein 3

The third selected sequence corresponds to the Osm1 protein (also referred to as MoHog1) from the rice blast fungus Magnaporthe oryzae (strain 70-15), identified by the locus tag MGG_01822. This protein is characterized by a highly conserved kinase domain that spans almost the entire sequence length, indicating its primary role in the phosphorylation of specific downstream substrates.

By homology with the well-studied Hog1 protein from Saccharomyces cerevisiae, it has been established that Osm1 is a Mitogen-Activated Protein Kinase (MAPK) essential for hyperosmotic stress response and pathogenicity (Dixon et al., 1999; Motoyama et al., 2014). Similar to its yeast ortholog, UniProt and sequence analysis indicate the presence of the signature TXY motif (specifically TGY in many fungal MAPKs). This motif contains the critical threonine and tyrosine residues whose dual phosphorylation activates the MAPK. Consequently, this motif is expected to be structurally conserved in our target sequence to allow for functional activation, despite the fact that the experimental structure for this specific protein remains unsolved.

In summary, Osm1 is a core component of the signaling pathway that enables M. oryzae to sense and adapt to changes in osmotic pressure, a capability that is crucial during the infection process. A null mutation of OSM1 was generated in M. grisea by targeted gene replacement, and the resulting mutants were sensitive to osmotic stress and showed morphological defects when grown under hyperosmotic conditions (Dixon et al., 1999). Furthermore, while Osm1 is indispensable for osmoregulation, it also plays a specialized role in the development of infection structures, as mutants exhibit reduced virulence and altered stress-related growth patterns (Motoyama et al., 2014)


Zhang et al 2014

Zhang, H., Zhao, Q., Guo, X., Guo, M., Qi, Z., Tang, W., Dong, Y., Ye, W., Zheng, X., Wang, P., & Zhang, Z. (2014). Pleiotropic function of the putative zinc-finger protein MoMsn2 in Magnaporthe oryzae. Molecular plant-microbe interactions : MPMI, 27(5), 446–460. https://doi.org/10.1094/MPMI-09-13-0271-R

Dixon etal 1999

Katherine P. Dixon, Jin-Rong Xu, Nicholas Smirnoff, Nicholas J. Talbot, Independent Signaling Pathways Regulate Cellular Turgor during Hyperosmotic Stress and Appressorium-Mediated Plant Infection by Magnaporthe grisea, The Plant Cell, Volume 11, Issue 10, October 1999, Pages 2045–2058, https://doi.org/10.1105/tpc.11.10.2045

Motoyama 2008
Motoyama, T., Ochiai, N., Morita, M. et al. Involvement of putative response regulator genes of the rice blast fungus Magnaporthe oryzae in osmotic stress response, fungicide action, and pathogenicity. Curr Genet 54, 185–195 (2008). https://doi.org/10.1007/s00294-008-0211-0

Zhang et al 2024

Zhang, H., Yang, J., Liu, M. et al. Early molecular events in the interaction between Magnaporthe oryzae and rice. Phytopathol Res 6, 9 (2024). https://doi.org/10.1186/s42483-024-00226-z
